coredata.dc:title: Multicolor emissive sulfur, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and their application in ion detection and solid lighting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Zhenxing
Wang, Wenhai
Wen, Ming
Zhang, Rui
Zhang, Qingmei
Li, Kun
Li, Jinhong
Yang, Wen
Zhou, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor emissive carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in many fields such as photoelectric display, light-emitting devices, and bioimaging. Such CDs by heteroatom doping of nitrogen elements assisted with various solvents has achieved recently, yet generally low efficiency in red-light region. Herein, we developed a facile route to synthesize multicolor-emissive CDs governed by a solvothermal method of tetrahydrofuran solvent. The maximum emission locates at 432, 510, and 584 nm with the absolute FL QYs up to 21.1%, 11.0%, and 55.4% for selected B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs, respectively. By means of the analysis on their PL spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and microstructures, the different graphitic degrees and surface states formed under the participation of S and N elements in as-prepared CDs determine the fluorescent color, and large sp2-conjugated domains within the YR-CDs is closely related to high QY. They, likewise, endow the YR-CDs with high sensitivity for ion detections of Ag+ and Fe3+, which was further illustrated to have different quenching mechanisms each other due to the affinity interaction with different surface groups of the CDs. High PL QYs of these CDs emission are beneficial to application in solid lighting, phosphors dried by these CDs solutions were mixed with PVP water solution to fabricate CDs/PVP films. The films exhibited stable fluorescence, and three phosphors were also mixed together in proportion to obtain white-light film with a CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.34), which demonstrates that these CDs are potentially fluorescent nanomaterial in the solid-state lighting field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N-codoped CDs
Multicolor emission
Ion detection
White-light film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparations and optical properties of the S, N-CDs
Morphologies and structures of the S, N-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work in China was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51502189 , 51871158 , 11705124 , 11704274 , and 11772177 ), the Foundation of the Outstanding Young Scholars of Shanxi Province, China ( 201801D211006 ), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province ( 201801D121084 and 201801D221121 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province ( 201701D221001 ), the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Innovative Research Team ( 1331KIRT ), Shanxi Graduate Education Innovation Project ( 2019SY501 ), the Jinzhong Science and Technology Innovation Carrier and Platform Construction Project ( P182002 ), and the School-Enterprise Technology Development Cooperation Project ( 201940 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of multicolor CDs from blue to yellow-red by solvothermal treatment (tetrahydrofuran, THF) of CA and GITC. (b) Photographs of eight CDs samples under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (c) Corresponding normalized PL emission spectra of the eight samples with maxima at 431, 465, 482, 501, 534, 560, 577, and 584 nm respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optical properties of the three typical CDs with blue, green, and yellow-red emissions. (a–c) UV–vis (Abs), PL excitation, and PL emission spectra of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b), and YR-CDs (c), the insets in (a–c) are the photographs of corresponding CDs solutions under daylight and optimal excitation irradiation. (d–f) The corresponding excitation-emission maps for B-CDs (d), G-CDs (e), and YR-CDs (f).
TEM images of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b), and YR-CDs (c), the insets in the TEM images are the corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and size distribution of G-CDs.
High-resolution C1s, N1s, S2p, and O1s XPS spectra of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b), and YR-CDs (c), respectively. Each band was deconvoluted following the literature.

The emission intensities (a) and comparison (b) of the YR-CDs solutions after adding different metal ions with the same concentration of 6.6 mM; The fluorescence intensities of the YR-CDs solutions with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (c) and Ag+ (e) at the excitation wavelength of 553 nm and the emission wavelength of 584 nm; Plot of the linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration C of Fe3+ (d) and Ag+ (f), which is derived from the results in (c) and (e) respectively. The Stern-Volmer fitting of YR-CDs-Fe3+ (0.66–660 μM) is performed in the inset of (e), yet the single linear fitting for YR-CDs-Ag+ (0.17–2.8 mM) in the inset of (f), respectively.
UV–vis absorbance and FT-IR spectra of the prepared YR-CDs as well as the changes after adding Ag+ and Fe3+, respectively.

(a) Normalized PL spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and YR-CDs PVP composite films under λ = 365 nm UV excitation and the corresponding PL photographs of three films in the insets. (b) PL spectrum of a white-light emission PVP composite film under λ = 365 nm UV excitation and the corresponding PL photographs is shown in the inset. (c) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra of the PVP films shown in (a) and (b).
Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve of the three-color CDs.
Content of various chemical bonds in B-CDs, G-CDs, and YR-CDs respectively.

head.ce:title: Multicolor emissive sulfur, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and their application in ion detection and solid lighting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor emissive carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in many fields such as photoelectric display, light-emitting devices, and bioimaging. Such CDs by heteroatom doping of nitrogen elements assisted with various solvents has achieved recently, yet generally low efficiency in red-light region. Herein, we developed a facile route to synthesize multicolor-emissive CDs governed by a solvothermal method of tetrahydrofuran solvent. The maximum emission locates at 432, 510, and 584 nm with the absolute FL QYs up to 21.1%, 11.0%, and 55.4% for selected B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs, respectively. By means of the analysis on their PL spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and microstructures, the different graphitic degrees and surface states formed under the participation of S and N elements in as-prepared CDs determine the fluorescent color, and large sp2-conjugated domains within the YR-CDs is closely related to high QY. They, likewise, endow the YR-CDs with high sensitivity for ion detections of Ag+ and Fe3+, which was further illustrated to have different quenching mechanisms each other due to the affinity interaction with different surface groups of the CDs. High PL QYs of these CDs emission are beneficial to application in solid lighting, phosphors dried by these CDs solutions were mixed with PVP water solution to fabricate CDs/PVP films. The films exhibited stable fluorescence, and three phosphors were also mixed together in proportion to obtain white-light film with a CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.34), which demonstrates that these CDs are potentially fluorescent nanomaterial in the solid-state lighting field.

This figure shows a new solvent-engineered strategy for the controlled synthesis of S, N-CDs with different colors by mild solvothermal decomposition of Guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) and citric acid (CA) dissolved in tetrahydrofuran (THF) solvents. Moreover, as-prepared YR-CDs is to explore the fluorescence selectivity of the CDs toward different metal ions due to the highest QYs, which is quenched the strongest in the presences of Ag+ and Fe3+ among various ions. Phosphors dried by these CDs are also mixed together in proportion to obtain white-light film, demonstrating that these CDs are potentially fluorescent nanomaterial in the solid state lighting field.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) with varied forms and types have attracted tremendous attention during the past decade. By virtue of their merits on optical properties [1,2], resistance to photobleaching [3,4], and biocompatibility [5,6], CDs have been proposed to be a promising candidate in various applications, such as photocatalysis [7], water splitting [8], biosensing [9], cell labeling [10], light-emitting diodes [11], etc. However, most of the as-prepared CDs exhibit strong emissions merely in the blue and green spectral regions [12–14], moreover, there is still controversial about the photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms of CDs and the formative processes of CDs also remain unclear [15–17], which seriously hinders the development and application in building a full-color-emitting spectrum based on the CDs materials, e.g., in the photoelectric fields of white-light-emitting diodes and full-color display.

Actually, most of the reported CDs only emitting blue and green lights are also able to emit long-wavelength lights (e.g. red light) under a green or yellow laser irradiation [18,19], yet such red-shift fluorescence of CDs is always accompanied by decreasing PL intensity due to the feature of excitation-dependent emission. The pace to search truly red-emissive CDs never quits, recently, main attention has turn to the heteroatom doping of nitrogen elements into CDs in the form of NH2 and NO2 groups to achieve the multicolor CDs assisted with all kinds of solvents. For example, Lin and colleagues [20] prepared multicolor photoluminescent CDs using three isomers of phenylenediamine with the assistance of ethanol solution, and being a pioneer in the field of full-color emission of CDs materials. With the addition of urea or L-glutamic acid as one of the precursors on the basis of phenylenediamines, multicolor CDs were also prepared with more excellent optical performance [21,22]. In addition, citric acid (CA) utilized as carbon source and N atomic dopant using above compounds, were similarly synthesized the CDs with multicolor emissions taking advantage of all kinds of solvents and their mixtures [23–25]. Coincidentally, both 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and 3,4,9,10-nitroperylene carrying several NO2 groups on the edge also achieved multicolor CDs with the highest fluorescence quantum yields (FL QYs) (80%) of green and red emission regions to date [26,27]. Nevertheless, what is especially worth mentioning is that multicolor emission of CDs are affected by many factors more than the N-doping, other heteroatoms doping is ever proposed as one of the mechanism for multicolor or full-color lights of CDs [28]. Looking back at the history of CDs materials, sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) elements co-doped means was used to be outstanding approach to obtain high FL QYs, thereby, promoting significantly the applications in related fields of CDs materials [29]. According to the band engineering theory, doping heteroatom S would further narrow the band gap in a N-doped CDs because S has a lower electronegativity than N. Inspired by the above, several reports have exhibited successful synthesis of S, N-doped CDs possessing orange or red emission besides of blue fluorescence, and their band energy states further narrowed down along with increasing S-doping into CDs, resulting in emission wavelength toward red region [30–32]. Unfortunately, these S, N-doped CDs did not show high FL QYs in the long-wavelength regions, hence, it is highly desirable and a great challenge to understand the optical properties and band structures of S, N-doped CDs in the process of long-wavelength emissions.

In this report, a one-step solvothermal synthetic route for preparation of S, N codoped CDs (S, N-CDs) with multicolor lights emission is developed using Guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) and CA with the aid of tetrahydrofuran (THF) solvents. The reason is that as-prepared CDs using these compounds via hydrothermal method emitted deep blue color light accompanied by the excitation-dependent emission, resulting in the yellow emission of CDs solution under a 520 nm laser irradiation before this work [33]. By regulating the reaction conditions, the maximum emission of the resulted CDs gradually shifts from blue to yellow-red light, nearly covering the entire visible light spectrum, and the highest FL QY reach up to 55.4% for YR-CDs. Moreover, we further perform the detection on metal ions using as-prepared YR-CDs without further chemical modifications or surface functionalization and also fabricate the CDs/PVP composite films by dissolving multicolor CDs into the Polyvinyl Pyrrolidone (PVP) water solutions. As expected, it shows the rich changes of fluorescence along with the additions of Ag+ and Fe3+, yet different mechanisms each other, several films including white-light film exhibit stable fluorescence, and all of the results will be elaborated on in Section III in detail.

GITC and Citric acid (CA) monohydrate were commercially obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. in China, THF and PVP powder were from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China), standard solutions of metal cations were diluted using ultrapure water from the respective salts of AlCl3.6H2O, CaCl2.2H2O, AgNO3, LiNO3, BaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, NaCl, KCl, MnSO4·H2O, ZnCl2, Fe (NO3)3·9H2O, Cd (NO3)2·4H2O, and CuSO4·5H2O, which were also purchased from several chemical works (Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. in China, Tianjin Guangfu technology development Co. Ltd., Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., and so on).

The multicolor S, N-CDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method under different conditions: the molar ratio of GT/CA = 8: 1, 4:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, and 1:8, 150, 170, 190, 210 °C for 6 and 9 h respectively were selected to perform synthetic experiments, which were dissolved in THF of 10 ml. After vigorously stirring and making solution clear at environment temperature, the mixed solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at the above conditions. With naturally cooling down to the room temperature, the obtained solutions containing the plenty of suspended particles were filtered with a 0.22 um membrane to remove the large particles. Then, the solutions were concentrated by using a rotary evaporator, and continue to be dried into powder using vacuum oven at 60 °C over 48 h.

Each of three color CDs (2–8 mg) was mixed in 2 ml of PVP solution (1 g PVP power dissolved into 10 ml water), then, which were continued to further mix by ultrasound for 1 h. After that, mixed solutions were poured respectively into square molds, and naturally dried for 2 days to obtain the final product. At last, single CDs/PVP film with 8 mg CDs possessed a better quality of color under 365 nm UV irradiation. Moreover, the white-light film with a weight ratio of B:G:YR = 2:1:1 and mixed with 2 ml of the same PVP solution was obtained by means of the adjustment scales repeatedly.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with an operating voltage of 200 kV on the JEM-2100, JEOL of Japan. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired from an ATR-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, ThermoFisher, America) with wavenumber range of 15–27,000 cm− 1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by a PHI-5000C ESCA system (PerkinElmer Inc., USA) using Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. UV–visible absorption spectrum was obtained by a TU-1901 UV/Visible spectrophotometer (Persee, Beijing) with the scanning range of 190–900 nm. PL spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and FL QYs of CDs were carried out on a FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England).

Aqueous solutions of all metal ions were prepared respectively with a concentration of 1 M, and then 20 μL of each solution was mixed with 2 ml of as-prepared YR-CDs solution (the highest FL QY in these CDs). After 30 min' standing, PL spectra of the supernate from mixed solutions were measured to record the fluorescence intensity. Likewise, different concentrations of Fe3+ and Ag+ solutions were also mixed in the standard YR-CDs solution for testing the influence of quenching (20 μl for ions; 3 and 2 ml for CDs solution, respectively), and incubated for 30 min once both ions solutions were added into YR-CDs solutions. Similarly, emissive spectra of these supernatants were measured using the excitation wavelength of 553 nm for comparison. The calibration curves were prepared by dissolving the same deionized water into the standard YR-CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations and optical properties of the S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a illustrates a strategy for the controlled synthesis of S, N-CDs with different colors by mild solvothermal decomposition of CA and GITC dissolved in THF. Both precursors were selected carefully and reaction conditions were optimized to obtain different colors taking into consideration of the synthesis of S, N-CDs in the processes of thermal treatment. A series of dehydration reaction from the different functional groups is helpful to form conjugated sp2-domains and dope N and S into these domains, accompanied by specific surface states possibly. After solvothermal treatment at different temperature, time and molar ratio of CA and GITC, CDs solutions in tones of colors from blue to yellow-red under 365 nm irradiation can be achieved indicating that the emission of the CDs can be tuned by reaction conditions and eight liquid samples as the models exhibit at Fig. 1b. Their PL emission maxima appear at 431, 465, 482, 501, 534, 560, 577, and 584 nm respectively, which nearly covers the entire visible spectrum as showed in Fig. 1c. Based on RGB 3-element color, three typical CDs samples (Fig. 1a) with blue, green, and yellow-red fluorescence are chosen for further investigation, and are labelled as B-CDs, G-CDs, and YR-CDs respectively according to their emissive colors. They were synthesized at conditions: CA/GITC = 1:8 at 150 °C, 9 h for B-CDs, CA/GITC = 8:1 at 210 °C, 6 h for G-CDs, and CA/GITC = 1:2 at 170 °C, 9 h for YR-CDs, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the three selected samples in Fig. 2a–c (blue spectra) exhibit significant absorption in the UV region, yet weak in the visible region. In the UV region, two peaks are observed by comparison between any two spectra, one is located at 231 nm, mainly derived from the π-π* transition of CC bands in carbon cores [22] and nearly remains stable for B-CDs, G-CDs, and YR-CDs, while the other one which is corresponded to the π-π* transition of CN bonds in carbon cores [22] undergoes dramatically redshift from ca. 250 nm in G-CDs to ca. 280 nm in YR-CDs, even there is unable to judge the exist in B-CDs, implying a gradually increasing content of N-doped aromatic rings in carbon cores [34]. In the visible region, G-CDs seems to display the best absorption at 300–400 nm, which is due to the n-π* transition of the conjugated CO/CN bonds [35]. YR-CDs continues to extend the absorption up to ca. 450 nm ascribable to the n-π* transition of the conjugated CN/CS bonds [36]. Fig. 2a–c also shows the results of optimal PL excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and YR-CDs respectively, as well as the corresponding photographs of the CDs solutions under daylight and optimal excitation as shown in their insets. Obviously, these spectra are composed of multi-peaks except for that of G-CDs, taking along the horizontal and vertical dash line to guide the optimal PL excitation and emission in Fig. 2d–f. For B-CDs, the maximum emissions gradually shift from 390 to 510 nm when the excitation wavelength changes from 330 to 400 nm (Fig. 2d), suggesting that B-CDs possess excitation-dependent emission properties, however, each of these emission peaks seems to exhibit excitation-independent emission behaviors. For G-CDs, there is a long emission range with a maximum peak at ≈520 nm within the range of excitation wavelength from 380 to 530 nm, and trace amount of red emission could be observed clearly until the wavelength up to 680 nm (Fig. 2e), indicating that G-CDs is excitation dependent emission. In the case of YR-CDs, the emission peaks mainly are from ≈580 nm besides that a part of weaker emissions appear nearby 530 nm within the excitation wavelength from 440 to 570 nm (Fig. 2f), both blue and green emissions are relatively weak even under short excitation wavelengths beginning with 350 nm, hence, it should be excitation-independent emission for YR-CDs. These results suggest that the multicolor emission of CDs prepared by this method can be customized by means of different reaction conditions. The absolute FL QYs of the CDs were measured to be 21.1% for B-CDs, 11.0% for G-CDs, and 55.4% for YR-CDs, respectively. As mentioned above, FL QY has been a key index for CDs in all kinds of optical applications, here, FL QY = 55.4% of the YR-CDs is relatively higher in this spectrum region compared with the other multicolor S, N-CDs [30–32].

The time-resolved PL decay curves of several CDs with different colors were measured to probe the fluorescence lifetime, and the results are exhibited in Fig. s1 and Table 1 , respectively. It can be seen that they can be fitted by a tri- or dual-exponential formula, involving two short-lived components of τ1 and τ2 for B-CDs, yet only a short-lived components τ2 for G-CDs and YG-CDs, and a long-lived component τ3 for the three CDs, which are assigned to the recombination of core states (molecular states) and surface states, respectively [37,38]. In accordance with the sequence of B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs, the τ3 proportion increases from 10.45% to 72.30% when the average lifetime increase from 2.93 to 7.07 ns, indicating that the main role of surface states in the radiative lifetime of these CDs strengthens with their PL redshift, which is differ from several reports on multicolor CDs [21,34,39]. The above results manifest that the prepared CDs possess excellent and unique optical properties, and their tunable PL emissions can be attributed to the synergistic effect between the core states (or molecular states) and surface states, especially the surface states that mainly dominate the color change of the CDs trend to long-wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structures of the S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the effect of reaction conditions on the PL multicolor emissions of the prepared CDs, morphological and structural analyses of the three typical CDs were carried out. Fig. 3a–c shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of as-prepared B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs respectively, and exhibits that these CDs are nearly spherical, yet not uniform, especially for B-CDs in Fig. 3a which emerges sparse and unequal-sized dots. To one's surprise, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of B-CDs in the inset of Fig. 3a cannot observe clearly the lattice fringes, suggesting that there is hardly any crystalline carbon structure into B-CDs. However, G-CDs is relatively uniform and well dispersed with a size distribution between 1.5 and 4.5 nm (average diameter ~2.8 nm) as shown in the particle size distribution histogram (bottom-right inset of Fig. 3b). Moreover, the HRTEM image of G-CDs in upper-right inset of Fig. 3b exhibits the well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, which agrees with that of in-plane lattice spacing of graphitic carbon (100 facet) [40]. For the YG-CDs, although size distribution cannot be identified due to severe aggregation of the prepared CDs as shown in Fig. 3c, the HRTEM image in upper-right inset of Fig. 3c shows entangled lattice fringes, thereby, implying very highly crystalline carbon structure into CDs, which results in appearing two types of lattice fringe distance fitted at 0.22 and 0.32 nm that are attributed to the d spacings of the graphitic (100) and (001) planes [25,40].

The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) and Fourier transfer infrared (FT-IR) were further used to investigate the elemental composition and surface states of these CDs. As shown in Fig. 4a–c, the high-resolution XPS results reveal the differences in the surfaces between the three samples. The high resolution scan of C 1s regions can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 284.3, 285.1, 286.4 and 288.7 eV, attributed to CC/CC, CN/CS/CO, CN/CN and NCO species, respectively. The N 1s bands revealed the presence of CNC (pyridinic N, 399.5 eV and pyrrolic N, 400.3 eV) and C3 N (graphite N, 401.3 eV) groups. The S 2p spectra have two main peaks at 163.7 and 164.9 eV sequentially, assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sites of CSC covalent bond in thiophene-type structure owing to the spin-orbit splitting [41,42]. The O 1s spectra also contain two peaks at 532.0 and 533.4 eV for CO and COH bands, respectively. Clearly, compared to the C and O elements, mutual content of N and S elements gradually increases according to the order of B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs, implying the importance of N and S doping in tuning the fluorescent color of these CDs. Moreover, the sp2 carbon (CC/CC) content also gradually increases from 37.70% (B-CDs) to 44.39% (YR-CDs) when the graphitic N content increases drastically from 6.26% (B-CDs) to 52.98% (YR-CDs) in Table 2 , manifesting an increase on the size of sp2-conjugated domains according to the color from blue to yellow-red in the forming process of these CDs, which agrees well with their increased graphitization degrees in HRTEM. For the FT-IR spectra of these CDs as shown in Fig. s2, all changes marked by shaded areas also imply to reduce dehydration and increase graphitization along with the color of CDs from blue to yellow-red. In brief, sp2-conjugated domains gradually increase resulting from the doping of N and S in as-prepared CDs with abundant oxygen/nitrogen/sulfur-containing surface groups, and their graphitic nitrogen/sulfur contents also increase concurrently when the PL emission redshift to yellow-red color.

One application of prepared YR-CDs is to explore the fluorescence selectivity of the CDs toward different metal ions due to the highest FL QY in these CDs, which is shown in Fig. 7a, b. Compared with the other metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of the YR-CDs was the strongest quenched in the presences of Ag+ and Fe3+, especially the quenching toward Fe3+ which nearly submerges into coordinate axis, suggesting that the CDs could be used to monitor Ag+ and Fe3+ in practical application, nevertheless it is also apparent that the two types of ions would interfere with each other in actual detection, thereby, the top priority using the CDs to monitor these ions is find out the difference of quenching rules between Ag+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5c, e, fluorescence intensities of the CDs solutions undergo decrease generally along with the adding Fe3+ and Ag+, and nearly disappear with the concentration up to C(Fe3+) = 6.6 mM and C(Ag+) = 6.0 mM respectively. Further analysis shows that the F0/F ratio slows down at the concentration of C(Fe3+) = 0.66 mM and C(Ag+) = 2.8 mM as shown in Fig. 5d, f respectively, revealing the different quenching mechanism existed before and after these concentrations. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5d, a satisfying linear relationship (F0/F = 11.254(±0.584) C + 1, R2 = 0.9988) is obtained in the range of 0.66–660 μM according to Stern-Volmer equation [43,44] and the limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/m) of CDs-Fe3+ system is as low as 1.12 μM. Moreover, the kq was calculated to be 1.59 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1 in view of the fluorescence lifetime of 7.07 × 10−9 s, which is obviously higher over the limiting diffusion constant Kdif of the biomolecule (Kdif = 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) [45] and fluorescence quenching was induced by complicated interactions between YR-CDs and Fe3+. Just as mentioned previously, there are various groups on the surface of these CDs, which have strong coordination interaction with Fe3+ to cause the quenching of fluorescence of CDs [46,47]. F0/F vs C also obtains a linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.17–3.0 mM for Ag+ in the inset of Fig. 5f, however, which is neither above dynamic quenching nor static quenching (Lineweaver–Burk equation) [43,44], only a linear equation of F0/F = 5.866C-6.771 (R2 = 0.9887) obtained with the limit of detection of 2.15 μM. Obviously, both Ag+ and Fe3+ have different quenching mechanisms toward YR-CDs solution, which can be further proved by the insight into the source of quenching according to the UV–vis absorbances, fluorescence lifetimes and FT-IR of YR-CDs after adding Ag+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 6a, the UV–vis absorption spectra are remarkably red-shifted concerning the π-π* transition peak of CC bands, probably resulted from the fact that some kinds of interactions between Ag+/Fe3+ and YR-CDs lead to the change of band gaps in YR-CDs, nevertheless, both ions possess different affinity pathway with YR-CDs in view of the new peak appearing at 300 nm after the addition of Ag+, while there is a more distinct red-shift of entire peak bands after adding Fe3+. As the peak related to the surface states of CDs at 420 nm disappeared after adding both ions, quenching process seems to clear and that is both ions have an effect with the surface groups on the CDs by the electron/energy transfer [48,49], which is also verified by the results of fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. s3 and Table 1). Obviously, the average lifetimes are dramatically decreased after adding both ions because the proportion of dominant τ3 which is related to surface states decreases from 72.30% to 43.12% (Fe3+) and 48.61% (Ag+) respectively. To further search the quenching difference toward both ions, FT-IR spectra of the YR-CDs with adding these ions were collected as shown in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that CN/CS/CON and NH groups, related to N and S elements doping in the form of NH, NH2 and SH, become weak with the addition of both ions, while a peak appears at 1621 cm− 1 which possibly origins from CN vibration and is only along with the CDs solution added into Fe3+, suggesting the quenching differences with Ag+ due to the selectivity to different surface groups. Additionally, pH effects in the detection toward both ions were also investigated as shown in Fig. s4, and there has been little noticeable change observed visually with adding both ions of different pH values, providing a promising method for the fabrication of photoluminescent S, N-CDs to monitor Ag+ and Fe3+ in practices.

Another application of the three-color CDs is to fabricate CDs/PVP composite films and try solid lighting as shown in Fig. 7 . Clearly, emission maxima of as-prepared films appear at 457, 507, and 595 nm under UV light of 365 nm in Fig. 7a, respectively, which undergo only a little change compared with those of the pure CDs (432, 510, and 584 nm for B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs, respectively). Likewise, a white-light emissive CDs/PVP film also achieved as the B-CDs, G-CDs, and YG-CDs are mixed by a weight ratio of 2:1:1 optimized via repeated experiments, and the corresponding PL spectrum exhibits three distinct emission peaks in Fig. 7b, suggesting that different CDs in color are able to coexist in the PVP film. Unlike the other CDs [50,51], these results confirm that there is no remarkable energy transfer or self-absorption between the three CDs as well as the CDs and PVP when these CDs are encapsulated in the PVP matrix [20,52]. Moreover, the white-light CDs/PVP film possesses a CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.34) in Fig. 7c, which is very close to the pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). Although more extensive characterizations are lacking for these films, these CDs/PVP composite films still have great potential for many sorts of lighting devices.

In summary, we proposed an approach toward regulating the reaction conditions of precursors to realize multicolor emissive CDs from GITC and CA, employing THF solvent in a solvothermal synthesis. The as-prepared CDs exhibited different fluorescence emission colors from blue to yellow-red, nearly covering the entire visible light spectrum with higher FL QY = 55.4% for YR-CDs. Their PL spectra showed a variational emission process that it is excitation-independent for B-CDs and YR-CDs, yet excitation-dependent for G-CDs. Detailed characterizations indicate N and S elements together participated in the dehydration and carbonization, and finally governed the microstructure related to different colors of CDs. Furthermore, we also tried to application in the detection on metal ions using as-prepared YR-CDs and fabricating the CDs/PVP composite films. The results showed the high sensitivity of fluorescence along with the additions of Ag+ and Fe3+, which was further illustrated to have different quenching mechanisms each other and provided a candidate as a sensing probe to monitor these metal ions in the industrial pollutants. Likewise, three monochromatic films exhibited stable fluorescence, and it is also able to mix them in proportion to obtain white-light film with a CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.34), suggesting their potential for applications in solid state lighting devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work in China was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51502189, 51871158, 11705124, 11704274, and 11772177), the Foundation of the Outstanding Young Scholars of Shanxi Province, China (201801D211006), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (201801D121084 and 201801D221121), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201701D221001), the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Innovative Research Team (1331KIRT), Shanxi Graduate Education Innovation Project (2019SY501), the Jinzhong Science and Technology Innovation Carrier and Platform Construction Project (P182002), and the School-Enterprise Technology Development Cooperation Project (201940).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117859.
